# Generated Code

This document describes what the source generator emits for a type annotated with `[NonBoxingUnion(...)]`, and the design decisions behind it.

## What gets generated

Given:

```csharp
using NonboxingUnion;

namespace MyApp;

[NonBoxingUnion(typeof(int), typeof(string))]
public partial struct IntOrString;
```

the generator produces a partial declaration (conceptually) like this:

```csharp
// <auto-generated/>
#nullable enable

namespace MyApp
{
    [global::System.CodeDom.Compiler.GeneratedCodeAttribute("NonboxingUnion.Generator", "...")]
    [global::System.Runtime.CompilerServices.Union]
    public partial struct IntOrString
        : global::System.Runtime.CompilerServices.IUnion, global::System.IEquatable<IntOrString>
    {
        private enum Discriminator : byte
        {
            None = 0,
            Int = 1,
            String = 2,
        }

        private Discriminator _discriminator;
        private int _int;
        private string? _string;

        public IntOrString(int value) { _discriminator = Discriminator.Int; _int = value; }
        public IntOrString(string value) { _discriminator = Discriminator.String; _string = value; }

        public object? Value => _discriminator switch
        {
            Discriminator.Int => _int,
            Discriminator.String => _string,
            _ => null,
        };

        public bool HasValue => _discriminator != Discriminator.None;

        public bool TryGetValue(out int value) { value = _int; return _discriminator == Discriminator.Int; }
        public bool TryGetValue(out string value) { value = _string!; return _discriminator == Discriminator.String; }

        // Equals / GetHashCode / == / != based on the active case.
    }
}
```

The implicit conversions from each case type and the exhaustive pattern matching are provided by the C# union language feature, which is enabled by the `[Union]` attribute and the `IUnion` implementation. The generator only supplies the storage and the access members.

## The discriminator

The active case is tracked by a private nested `enum Discriminator`. The member `None = 0` is reserved for the uninitialized (`default`) state, so a `default(T)` reports `HasValue == false` and a `Value` of `null`.

### Backing type

The discriminator uses the **smallest unsigned integer** that can represent every case plus the reserved `None` value. The number of values it must represent is `caseCount + 1`.

| Cases (+ `None`) | Backing type |
| --- | --- |
| up to 256 | `byte` |
| up to 65,536 | `ushort` |
| up to 4,294,967,296 | `uint` |
| more | `ulong` |

In practice almost every union uses `byte`. Picking the smallest type keeps the struct as compact as possible.

> *Note :notebook:*
> - A union must declare at least one case type; an empty `[NonBoxingUnion]` produces nothing.

## Storage layout

Each case is stored in its own private field. Crucially, fields are **not** overlapped:

- Value-type cases are stored inline, so no boxing or heap allocation occurs when a value type is placed into or read out of the union.
- `[StructLayout(LayoutKind.Explicit)]` / `[FieldOffset]` are deliberately *not* used. Overlapping fields would shrink the struct, but it pessimises the JIT, prevents inline storage of fields that contain managed references, and is unsound when cases hold reference types. Predictable, non-overlapping storage is preferred over minimal size.

This means the size of the struct is roughly the sum of its case fields plus the discriminator. If you need a different size/performance trade-off, that is a deliberate non-goal of this generator.

## Case type handling

### Value types

Stored in a field of the exact type and returned directly from `TryGetValue`, with no boxing.

### Reference types

The storage field is declared **nullable** (e.g. `string?`) because it is left `null` while a different case is active, which keeps nullable reference analysis happy. The case type itself and the constructor parameter stay **non-nullable**, and the `TryGetValue` out parameter uses the null-forgiving operator so the non-nullable out contract is preserved when the case is active.

### Nullable value types

A nullable value-type case, e.g. `typeof(int?)`, collapses to its underlying type (`int`) for both the case type and the `TryGetValue` out parameter, matching the C# union specification. The underlying `Nullable<T>` is unwrapped via `GetValueOrDefault()` when read out.

### Nested types

Case types are emitted using their fully-qualified names, so deeply nested and namespaced case types are handled without ambiguity.

## Equality

The generated struct implements `IEquatable<T>` and overrides `Equals(object?)`, `GetHashCode()`, `==`, and `!=`. Two unions are equal when they have the same active case **and** the stored values for that case are equal (compared with `EqualityComparer<T>.Default`). Two `default` unions are equal. Implementing equality also satisfies the analyzer rule that value types should override equality (`CA1815`).

## File and member naming

- **Hint name** (the generated file name) is `[<namespace>.][<containing types>.]<TypeName>.g.cs`, so unions with the same name in different namespaces or containing types do not collide.
- **Discriminator members** are derived from each case type's name, sanitised to a valid identifier and de-duplicated with a numeric suffix when two cases would otherwise produce the same name.
- **Storage fields** are the camel-cased discriminator member prefixed with `_` (e.g. case `String` -> field `_string`).

## Requirements recap

> *Note :notebook:*
> - The annotated type must be a `partial struct`, and every type that contains it must also be `partial`.
> - The target runtime must provide `System.Runtime.CompilerServices.UnionAttribute` and `IUnion`, with the C# union language feature available.
